item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously underrated. One of my favourite SCPs. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This takes a simple idea and goes all the way. I love it.

I like this skip a lot, so I went ahead and gave it a +1. My only question is, if they can't manage to kill him or help him escape, why not put him into a medically-induced coma of some kind. Or, heck, they were able to stop his heart, why not just keep it stopped? I'd imagine death is more preferable to what this guy is going through.

Or, at the very least, get this poor man a portable DVD player and a couple dozen complete-edition box sets of long-running TV shows. That should help.

Jokes aside, great work, well done.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Good point! I added a footnote clarifying the multiplicative factor.

This is a great article. Project Timaeus is such a creative idea, hell, the whole object is a such a creative idea. I love it.

One minor suggestion. In your author's note, you say that:

this is someone who gets around a week of human interaction per every couple thousand years.

That is not the impression of the magnitude of the time distortion that I got at all. You don't specify how much the time is distorted by in the article, only saying that it's a "significant multiplicative factor," so I thought that time was sped up by 10x or 20x, but according to your note, you're trying to express that it's being sped up by like 50,000x. So, I would recommend that you give the multiplicative factor a range, similarly to how you specified a range between the distortions, or specify how much time -2 usually experiences per distortion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I was honestly on the fence about it myself, I think I'll remove it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have an opinion that the
+ Spoilers
- tough love feedback

white text is completely unnecessary and even may detract from the impact of the rest of the piece. It felt tacked on and tacky to me. Not sure how it played out for everybody else, though.

item.title: Re: History of the article
item.content:encoded: Just a heads-up that I made some minor quality improvements recently. Mostly formatting and tweaking a couple words here and there, but I figured it was worth the announcement nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. I meant to say that I prefer the article as-is, compared to a hypothetical version where you put the [spoiler] in the article explicitly. Discussion pages are fair game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point on the Death of the Author, I'll remove the spoiler. Thanks for the feedback and I'm glad you liked it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. It explores a lot of interesting and distinct ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is nice.

The D-class is good, and a dark parallel to characters such as 11424, where in this case the rapport is still there but the exploration isn't a happy ending. I also like that you avoided making him a one-dimensional "arrrghhh i'm angry at the Foundation" cliche.

Project Timaeus is insanely creative in concept, kudos for that.

Personally, I prefer the article without the spoiler, guess that's Death of the Author. My impression when reading was that the effect would happen through purely nonanomalous means; Elmsway isn't going to be there forever, whether via transfer, amnesticization, or even plain old death. One day there'll be a containment breach, or an attack on the Site, or a K-class scenario, or just the simple ravages of bureaucracy, and 4903-2 will eventually be forgotten and alone. Forever.

Excellent work.

item.title: History of the article
item.content:encoded:
This ended up being pretty long for a discussion post, so the history is in the collapsible for those who are curious.

Open textdump
- Close textdump

Before the final draft, before the crappy first draft, before I even had an account on this site I had a thought: what would happen to somebody stuck forever in a single room? This concept developed to the idea of an empty pocket dimension where immortality is guaranteed and escape is impossible, along with the temporal distortions to have the subject both experience a near eternity and be able to communicate with the outside world.

The main differences (besides basic quality increases) between the current version and the original version were that SCP-4903 was originally a painting and the temporal distortions were under the control of SCP-4903-2. I changed it from a painting because weird paintings are boring and done to death, and I changed the distortions to something chaotic and uncontrollable to fit with the feel of the article.

Researcher Elmsway was a result of my desire to hold a more concrete foil to the Foundation. I had the idea in mind that the effect of SCP-4903 would create a living testament to the failings of the Foundation method of sending D-Class on early exploratory missions into anomalies, and initially Elmsway was just the name of the device that was used in the incident log. I ended up liking the name and kept him as the Foundation side of the story, a scientist who can see the failings of his work but also who knows that there was no other feasible way to test the danger of new anomalies to humans. I surmised that SCP-4903-2 would be able to come to the same conclusion. I also thought that SCP-4903-2 being friendly and joking while holding a mostly nihilistic outlook would be the most effective for the story. Remember, this is someone who gets around a week of human interaction per every couple thousand years.

- Close textdump

I also added a hint of another concept, but I made sure the article worked without it. I would recommend opening the spoiler only after reading the article completely.

There's another effect at play here, but on the advice of Weryllium I'm removing the spoiler for it so you'll have to figure it out yourself ;)
